issue_title: Create an Enum-range to EEP.xml.
issue_body:
We really need a new kind of Enum-variable, as VLD messages in particular seem to like to set enums up to 7 bits :S

The way I'd see this working is something like:
```xml
<enum description="Output value" shortcut="OV" offset="17" size="7">
  <item description="Output value 0% or OFF" value="0" />
  <item description="Output value {value}% or ON" start="1" end="100" />
  <item description="Not used" start="101" end="126" />
  <item description="output value not valid / not set" value="127" />
</enum>
```
So in essence, we would need a new `item` parser, which takes into account start and end of range and formats description accordingly. `value` -type isn't useful here, as it provides no possibility for error cases, and further more the enum really should correspond to an integer value instead of a float.

That functionality should correspond to what I'm currently (forced to) implementing:
```xml
<enum description="Output value" shortcut="OV" offset="17" size="7">
  <item description="Output value 0% or OFF" value="0" />
  <item description="Output value 1% or ON" value="1" />
  <item description="Output value 2% or ON" value="2" />
  <item description="Output value 3% or ON" value="3" />
  <item description="Output value 4% or ON" value="4" />
...
  <item description="Output value 99% or ON" value="99" />
  <item description="Output value 100% or ON" value="100" />
  <item description="Not used" value="101" />
  <item description="Not used" value="102" />
  <item description="Not used" value="103" />
...
  <item description="Not used" value="125" />
  <item description="Not used" value="126" />
  <item description="output value not valid / not set" value="127" />
</enum>
```

issue_title: Add support for DPUB
Experiment State
issue_body:
When writing MATLAB code, I would tend to manage experiment state like:

```matlab
state = 'pretrial'
while true
    switch state
        case 'pretrial'
            % draw something every frame of pretrial
            if condition % block state change if conditional is false
                state = 'intrial'
            end
        case 'intrial'
            if enter_intrial
                % do something once
                enter_intrial = false
            end
            % draw something every frame of intrial
...
``` 

An alternative in python could be to use the [transitions](https://github.com/pytransitions/transitions) package. This would accomplish at least two things:

1. Clean up state loop (abstracted elsewhere)
2. Handle single-shot things more cleanly

Not-so-minimal example:

https://gist.github.com/aforren1/32fe8192bc1048a422629f340b069b44

issue_title: Digest authentication rejected due to invalid header
issue_body:
The header returned by the server when the Digest protocol is used is not formatted correctly.
VLC rejects the authentication header due to invalid algorithm "MD5", when it should be MD5.
Please see  [RFC2617](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2617#section-3.2.1)

Note that I first thought this is a VLC issue, until they proved me wrong in reading the spec. The algorithm name should not be quoted. Actual quotes are escaped in the spec as <">.

Current format of authentication header:
    Digest realm="/", nonce="<some_nonce>", algorithm="MD5", qop="auth"

Should be:
   Digest realm="/", nonce="<some_nonce>", algorithm=MD5, qop="auth"

issue_title: Installation does not happen smoothly for Jupyter (iPython 4)
issue_body: Post 3.x iPython has been split off into a variety of pieces, handling installation here is a bit problematic. There is not a clear way to handle this with `setuptools` ATM. ( https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/issues/434/handling-either-or-requirement ) However, this problem has showed up in other projects before. Likely, we can try to import iPython and see if it is < 4.0.0. If so, we can assume we have everything. If it can't be imported or it is 4.x, we will add more install requirements to solve this problem.

Placemark>ExtendedData>Data without value crash the parser

Even if value is mandatory, it happen's that value tag is missing in a Data tag.

Exemple:
```
			<Placemark>
				<name>Ligne Analytics</name>
				<styleUrl>#msn_ylw-pushpin10</styleUrl>
				<ExtendedData>
					<Data name="title"/>
					<Data name="description"/>
					<Data name="stroke">
						<value>#a3e46b</value>
					</Data>
					<Data name="stroke-width">
						<value>8</value>
					</Data>
					<Data name="stroke-opacity">
						<value>1</value>
					</Data>
				</ExtendedData>
				<LineString>
					<coordinates>x,y</coordinates>
				</LineString>
			</Placemark>
```

Problem's here:
https://github.com/cleder/fastkml/blob/master/fastkml/kml.py#L1441
```
self.value = element.find('%svalue' % self.ns).text
```

find() returns null, then null.text rise an AttributeError
```

```

issue_title: Progress Bar Fix
issue_body:
In the file backtest.py in the Backtest class, when the progress bar is initialized, it should be 

`bar = pyprind.ProgBar(len(self.dates)-1, title=self.name, stream=1)`
 
not

`bar = pyprind.ProgBar(len(self.dates), title=self.name, stream=1)`

Since the backtest has NA's as the first row, and update is only called on the real data rows.

This will let the progress bar actually finish and display the total time elapsed once it completes.

-Brian

issue_title: fitsio write too slow on some file systems
issue_body:
We recently encountered a weird problem writing new fits files (DECam images) using fitsio 0.9.7 on NCSA Blue Waters (which uses a Lustre file system). Reading and DECam CCD image (80Mb) takes no time, but writing a copy to a new file, takes a about 3 minutes. Repeating the same exercise using pyfits instead takes only ~5 seconds.

A similar delay is observed in our cosmology cluster at NCSA (which uses GPFS file system). Using fitsio it takes ~35 sec and pyfits only 6s. 
A quick inspection using strace indicates that fitsio is doing a lot of read() while doing write()

issue_title: fastq-dump parameters are not optimal
issue_body:
I run fastq-dump with the following parameters:
```
 /opt/sratoolkit/fastq-dump --skip-technical --gzip --readids --read-filter pass --dumpbase --split-files --clip ${file}
``` (at https://edwards.sdsu.edu/research/fastq-dump/ there are good explanations for need in some of them). While default geoparse has
```
cmd = "fastq-dump --split-files --gzip %s --outdir %s %s"
 ```
That creates some problems. For instance, if I do not have --readids and use paired sra, I get two files with ideas that are the same, that creates problem for downstream analysis. If I do not provide --skip-technical, then I get some technical Illumina reads that have nothing to do with biology ( like Application Read Forward -> Technical Read Forward <- Application Read Reverse - Technical Read Reverse. ) --read-filter pass allows to get read of multiple N-s in reads

issue_title: Sharing cassettes between Python 2 and 3 is tricky (when matching on body)
issue_body:
I have a test that needs to match on the request body, because it sends a POST. This works fine in a pure Python 2 world, and it works fine in a pure Python 3 world. Things get tricky when you need the same code to work under both Python 2 and 3.

Specifically, if I first run my tests under Python 2, then my cassette files store bodies like this:
```
interactions:
- request:
    body: 'hello world

      how are you today?'
[...]
```

If I then run my test under Python 3, it fails because the bodies don't match:
```
E
======================================================================
ERROR: test_echo_with_body (tests.test_echo.TestEchoClient)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/gward/src/python-renesys-apis/tests/test_echo.py", line 24, in test_echo_with_body
    response = call_echo(body)
  File "/home/gward/src/python-renesys-apis/tests/test_echo.py", line 10, in call_echo
    return urllib.request.urlopen(req)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/urllib/request.py", line 161, in urlopen
    return opener.open(url, data, timeout)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/urllib/request.py", line 463, in open
    response = self._open(req, data)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/urllib/request.py", line 481, in _open
    '_open', req)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/urllib/request.py", line 441, in _call_chain
    result = func(*args)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/urllib/request.py", line 1210, in http_open
    return self.do_open(http.client.HTTPConnection, req)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/urllib/request.py", line 1185, in do_open
    r = h.getresponse()
  File "/var/lib/venv/ren3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/vcr/stubs/__init__.py", line 236, in getresponse
    self.cassette.record_mode)
nose.proxy.CannotOverwriteExistingCassetteException: No match for the request (<Request (POST) http://gerg.ca/echo.cgi>) was found. Can't overwrite existing cassette ('echo_with_body.yaml') in your current record mode ('once').
-------------------- >> begin captured logging << --------------------
vcr.cassette: DEBUG: Entered context for cassette at echo_with_body.yaml.
vcr.stubs: DEBUG: Got <Request (POST) http://gerg.ca/echo.cgi>
vcr.matchers: DEBUG: Requests <Request (POST) http://gerg.ca/echo.cgi> and <Request (POST) http://gerg.ca/echo.cgi> differ according to the following matchers: [(False, <function body at 0x7fb22cb21e18>)]

The workaround appears to be to regenerate my cassettes under Python 3. Then they look like this:

* if that is the best we can do, it really should be documented!

issue_title: No EOF checks after calling `self.remote_conn.recv()`
issue_body:
Both `_read_channel()` and `_read_channel_expect()` do not check for EOF after calling `self.remote_conn.recv()`.  This causes a connection which has closed to raise

`NetMikoTimeoutException("Timed-out reading channel, pattern not found in output: {}".format(pattern))`

This isn't exactly desired behavior as it makes it hard to discern whether the command just took too long or the connection is dead.  Most of the time, a subsequent call to `self.send_command()` will cause paramiko to raise `socket.error("Socket is closed")`, but I have ran into a case where the socket only half closed, so the `socket.error` never got raised.

I can fix this, but I'd like guidance on the preferred way to signal this error to the user.  `EOFError`?  A netmiko exception?  Something else?

issue_title: memory leak seen while using getServices() call in a loop
issue_body:
Hi Ian,

I am trying to connect to a BLE sensor device from RaspberryPi and get information by reading the characteristics on it. All works fine with the below skeleton code except that I see a small memory leak when used over a period of time. 
Below is the code that I use to connect and read from the characteristic. I am able to get the data. On my RaspberryPi I see that the memory increases by 0.1% for every 220+ counts of the while loop.

When checked for memory leak it was seen that only the lbleServlist = lPerHandle.getServices()     function was causing the leak and I am deleting the list below as del lbleServlist[:] since I am using pythin2.7 

Please suggest what might be the cause for the leak using the getServices() call on python 2.7.

Thanks
Sudhindra






def fble_connect(lMACaddr,lType,lThreadNum):
    global mutex
    
    while True:
    # start the BLE part here
        mutex.acquire()
        try:
            lPerHandle = btle.Peripheral(lMACaddr, lType)
            lbleServlist = lPerHandle.getServices()        #<----> Memory leak <---->
            lSensorService = lPerHandle.getServiceByUUID(btle.UUID("ae27a3a0-6ef8-46f2-a3ea-02d113fe1f2e"))
            lChArr = lSensorService.getCharacteristics()

            lCharacteristic = lChArr[0]
            lSensorArr = lCharacteristic.read()

            lPerHandle.disconnect()

            del lPerHandle
            del lChArr[:]
            del lbleServlist[:]
            del lSensorService
            del lSensorArr
            
            mutex.release()
            time.sleep(1)
        except:
            if DEBUG:
                print("Unable to connect yet....thread %d", lThreadNum)
            mutex.release()
            time.sleep(1)

issue_title: Path component of (request-target) header field value should be lower-cased?
issue_body:
The IETF draft spec (ver 09) says:
```
       If the header field name is `(request-target)` then generate the
       header field value by concatenating the lowercased :method, an
       ASCII space, and the :path pseudo-headers (as specified in
       HTTP/2, Section 8.1.2.3 [6]).
```
This could be read to mean either that just the `:method` pseudo-header should be lower-cased or that both `:method` and `:path` should be lower-cased. The service that I'm working with definitely seems to expect both to be lower-cased. 

Essentially, I think this line in `utils.py`:
```
            signable_list.append('%s: %s %s' % (h, method.lower(), path))
```
should be changed to:
```
            signable_list.append('%s: %s %s' % (h, method.lower(), path.lower()))
```

issue_title: Times for events in record()
issue_body:
Could you add the possibility to know when the keyboard events occured during the recording ?
So maybe add smth like this :

def start_recording(recorded_events_queue=None, times_list = False):
    """
    Starts recording all keyboard events into a global variable, or the given
    queue if any. Returns the queue of events and the hooked function.
    Use `stop_recording()` or `unhook(hooked_function)` to stop.
    """
    recorded_events_queue = recorded_events_queue or _queue.Queue()

    global _recording
    
    if times_list:
        times_list = []
        def event():
            recorded_events_queue.put()
            times_list.append(time.time())
        _recording = (recorded_events_queue, times_list, hook(event))
        
    else:
        _recording = (recorded_events_queue, hook(recorded_events_queue.put))
        
return _recording

issue_title: Improve efficiency for bipartite matching?
issue_body:
Right now we rely on an [all-pairs distance calculation](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/util.py#L718) between reference and estimate events.  If you have a large set on either end (say, 10^5), this is not a great idea.  [Tagging @lostanlen for bringing this up.]

Since these are all comparing 1-dimensional objects, we could accelerate this from `O(n*m)` (for `n` reference and `m` estimates) to `O(m * log(n))` by sorting both arrays and doing bisection-search (`np.searchsorted`) for each event.  For each estimate event `t`, we can bisect-left the reference for `t-window` and bisect-right for `t+window` to get a slice `u:v`, constituting all hits.  This can be done quickly in numpy as follows:

```python
ref_sort = np.sort(refs)
left_hits = np.searchsorted(ref_sort, est - window, side='left')
right_hits = np.searchsorted(ref_sort, est + window, side='right')

# hits for est[i] are ref_sort[left_hits[i]:right_hits[i]]
```

issue_title: Accept column indexes greater than the length of lines
issue_body:
See the related issue raised for python-language-server: https://github.com/palantir/python-language-server/issues/193

`jedi` prevents completion requests from being issued for column indexes greater than the length of a line.

```python
        if not (0 <= column <= line_len):
            raise ValueError('`column` parameter is not in a valid range.')
```

This validation makes it hard for python-language-server to implement the Language Server Protocol correctly, which states that column positions greater than the length of a line must be treated as if the line length was given instead. I recommend checking if the `column` value is `>= 0`, and using `column = min(column, line_len)` instead.

This kind of problem is common in Vim where the cursor rests on the end of word, like a column position of `4` for a cursor at the end of a line with `abc` like `abc|`. Other language servers for other languages might use that information to differentiate between `ab|c` and `abc|`.

labels=hostname:`hostname`

issue_title: Value for free disk space is counterintuative on ext file systems
issue_body:
The value for free disk space is counterintuitive on file systems like ext2/3/4, possibly others as well.
By default, 5% of the disk space is reserved for root, at least on ext file systems.

At the moment, glances includes these 5% when showing the free space. But since this space is reserved no one but root can actually use it. Therefore it would make more sense to show only the remaining usable space.

Glances uses psutil to get the disk usage and psutil knows about the free space without the reserved blocks. To fix this I would suggest to change the line 132 in glances_fs.py to
```python
fs_current = {
    ...
    'free': fs_usage.free,
    ...}
```
https://github.com/nicolargo/glances/blob/master/glances/plugins/glances_fs.py#L132

issue_title: The syntax for disabling multiple warnings in one line is too strict

As of now, the `# noqa: ...` statement disables only the first warning when the corresponding codes are separated by `', '` (with a space), e.g. the following will only disable `Q440`:

`# noqa: Q440, Q447, Q448, Q449`

-- which might be confusing if you don't know that spaces are not allowed (they probably should be). The following still disables all the warnings:

`# noqa: Q440,Q447,Q448,Q449`

issue_title: deep learning learning rate consistency across optimizers
issue_body:
for some reason, keras optimizers have wildly different learning rates. 

some of them default to the values in the original papers, which might work well on small datasets where you can iterate for forever, but probably aren't optimal on a large dataset where you might only get in 6 iterations total. 

look into making learning rate consistent across optimizers. probably set it at the SGD rate of `0.01`. ideally in the far future this will be configurable based on dataset size, but that's well past MVP.

issue_title: Speed Test CLI doesn't match ping on Raspberry PI
issue_body:
I just tried to run `speedtest-cli` on my raspberry PI, and I found that the results don't match ping:

```bash
pi@raspberrypi:~$ speedtest-cli
Retrieving speedtest.net configuration...
Testing from O2 (...)...
Retrieving speedtest.net server list...
Selecting best server based on ping...
Hosted by bc-networks (Ludwigsburg) [...]: 875.092 ms
...

pi@raspberrypi:~$ ping -c 1 speedtest1.bc-networks.de
PING speedtest1.bc-networks.de (176.9.58.37) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from speedtest1.bc-networks.de (176.9.58.37): icmp_seq=1 ttl=54 time=26.4 ms
```

I think this is because your timing method is recording a lot of slow client-side activities on the low-spec'd raspberry PI. Since the goal of the script is to test the ISP speed, I think it should be immune from any client specifics.

Here is the code I think is responsible:

```python
start = timeit.default_timer()
h.request("GET", urlparts[2], headers=headers)
r = h.getresponse()
total = (timeit.default_timer() - start)
```

Would you consider switching out `httplib` to calling through to `ping` directly?

issue_title: Deprecate mutability of Settings objects
issue_body:
So Settings objects are currently mutable. It's even part of their recommended usage pattern. For example, from the documentation:

```pycon
>>> from hypothesis import Settings
>>> s = Settings()
>>> s.max_examples
200
>>> Settings.default.max_examples = 100
>>> t = Settings()
>>> t.max_examples
100
>>> s.max_examples
200
```

It's undocumented, but you can even assign Settings.default (historically this has not worked correctly, but it started working correctly in a recent version):

```
>>> from hypothesis import Settings
>>> Settings().max_examples
200
>>> Settings.default = Settings(max_examples=10)
>>> Settings().max_examples
10
```

I think this mostly leads to confusion, and it's particularly confusing in light of #154, in which @Bachmann1234 introduced a much better way to change settings globally.

My proposal is that all of these assignments should now start to emit deprecation warnings, with the recommendation of using the new profiles mechanism and/or local settings objects, with the goal of settings becoming immutable in Hypothesis 2.0.

Does anyone have any use cases they feel are not well represented by this? Votes aye or nay?

issue_title: Strace-like functionality
issue_body:
Ruby has an very interesting tracer tool: https://github.com/tmm1/rbtrace/

Hunter could have something like that:

* IPC with SysV queues (tracer would write to the queue, and external process can read from it).

   * Now there's the question of POSIX queues. Or not. They aren't supported on OSX: http://semanchuk.com/philip/posix_ipc/#platforms

* Need external activation of tracer. There are two ways to do it:

  * On demand activation:
   
    * Tracer is installed from signal handler. 
    * Signal handler is installed from pth file.
    * Only works for mainthread. Installing tracers in other threads means patching some PyThreadState structs from C code (dangerous)

  * Passive tracer:

    * Tracer is always installed from pth file but I doesn't do anything until someone connects to queue.
    * Need to detects connections to queue. Or use signal to start pushing stuff in queue?
    * Overhead should be ok for CPython, but what about PyPy?

issue_title: Rendezvous should not send list of in-use nameplates to clients
issue_body:
In a high volume relay, an attacker looking to intercept files can use the `_list` function to find nameplates waiting for a receiver, and use them to attempt file interception. On a high traffic relay, say 2-3 transfers a second, you have about a 30% chance of an intercept over the course of 2 hours. 

If we prevent auto-complete on nameplates however, an attacker would have to guess nameplates to find one with a sender listening. Moreover, if they are rapidly polling low nameplates, legitimate users would be less likely to receive those nameplates from the server, especially if the rate limiting is implemented on nameplates.

issue_title: Ordering with multiple files
issue_body:
Use case:
* creaet a test file "test_example1.py"
* Mark order from 1 to 5 for different test_methods 
* Now create another test file "test_example2.py"
* Mark order from 1 to 5 for different test_methods
* py.test executes order 1 from both the files first even the test_method names are diffrent
* And it goes in similar pattern, executing order=1 in all files then goes to order=2 in all files etc.

It becomes very difficutl when we have more than 100 test case , If any one test case is added in single file we need to change the order number in all test case files.
More info in "http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35909656/pytest-ordered-plugin-doesnt-work-with-combination-of-multiple-files"

issue_title: Format error in python 2.6
issue_body:
Here's the trace on login route.  It actually seems to happen anytime .format() is used.  For some reason it works in Python 2.7 but not the Python 2.6.6 shipped in CentOS6.  The issue is that Python 2.6 expects a key in the {} for format (https://docs.python.org/2/whatsnew/2.6.html#pep-3101-advanced-string-formatting).  

Here's the trace:
```
127.0.0.1 - - [15/Oct/2014 17:17:40] "GET /auth-cas/login/ HTTP/1.1" 500 -
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1836, in __call__
    return self.wsgi_app(environ, start_response)
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1820, in wsgi_app
    response = self.make_response(self.handle_exception(e))
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1403, in handle_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1817, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1477, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.handle_user_exception(e)
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1381, in handle_user_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1475, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.dispatch_request()
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1461, in dispatch_request
    return self.view_functions[rule.endpoint](**req.view_args)
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask_cas/routing.py", line 35, in login
    flask.url_for('.login', _external=True))
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask_cas/cas_urls.py", line 72, in create_cas_login_url
    ('renew',renew),
  File "/home/sawilliams/auth-cas/env/lib/python2.6/site-packages/flask_cas/cas_urls.py", line 46, in create_url
    url = urljoin(url, '?{}'.format(urlencode(list(query))))
ValueError: zero length field name in format
```

I should have a pull request that will fix this soon.  Basically, since we're only using format to cast one string, using %s vs .format() is trivial, but will allow for better compatibility as it's still valid in py3.  Replacing this with a %s cast makes it work on EL6.

Scott

issue_title: VPC NAT Gateways limit is per-AZ
issue_body:
The VPC "NAT Gateways" limit merged in #204 and released in 0.5.1 is actually [per-AZ, not per-region](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/VPC_Appendix_Limits.html#vpc-limits-gateways). That code will need to be changed to add usage for each AZ separately.

This is considered a bug, as the limit isn't being evaluated properly.

The AWS docs call this limit "NAT gateways per Availability Zone" and say, "A NAT gateway in the pending, active, or deleting state counts against your limit." (default of 5, per AZ).

I'm torn on whether I should just fix the calculation issue and release this as 0.5.2, or fix the calculation and rename the limit from "NAT gateways" to "NAT gateways per Availability Zone", which would require holding it for 0.6.0.

issue_title: python-zstandard incompatible with pypy
issue_body:
Greetings,

I ran into another issue when I started using python-zstandard with pypy.  The module builds, but it fails at runtime because the bytes objects can't be resized.  My workaround has been to change decompress2 to return a list of strings (iterate over -- decompression, PyBytes_FromStringAndSize on the output, PyList_Append with the result, and Py_DECREF the result) instead of growing a single string.  I suspect this change makes things slower (not to mention incompatible), but it was worth it for me to get pypy working quickly.

issue_title: Depth channel socket update messages
issue_body:
Hello guys,

 I thinks there's an issue on the way depth socket messages are handled by the DepthCacheManager. Playing around with it for several days and storing the values in a time series database, I noticed after some restarts that sometimes, either the best bid or best ask would not move until the next restart. I believe it's due to 2 things: 

1. 

The start_depth_socket() method of the BinanceSocketManager listens by default to the [Diff.Depth Stream endpoint](https://github.com/binance-exchange/binance-official-api-docs/blob/master/web-socket-streams.md#diff-depth-stream) and not to the [Depth Websocket EndPoint](https://www.binance.com/restapipub.html#depth-wss-endpoint) described on the api doc of Binance website. The difference between those 2 is that the diff depth stream endpoint provides a first_update_id and a last_update_id ('U' and 'u' in the json response). The DepthCacheManager does not handle this first_update_id as described [here](https://github.com/binance-exchange/binance-official-api-docs/blob/master/web-socket-streams.md#how-to-manage-a-local-order-book-correctly)

2. 

From the constructor of the DepthCacheManager: 
`self._start_socket()`
`self._init_cache()`

==> what can happen is that the self._start_socket() method takes longer than the self._init_cache(), in that case, the sate of the order book would not be consistent between the one returned by the rest api call and the one returned by the websocket. For example, _init_cache() could return a LastUpdateId = 1, and _start_socket() could return a first message with 'U' = 3 and 'u' = 4, which would mean we did not process the update 2.
So I think it is necessary to add code to wait for the websocket to have received at least 1 message before starting the rest call to get the state of the current order book, and also start processing websocket from U == LastUpdateId +1 (and not U <= LastUpdateId +1).

Also, another weird stuff I noticed (this time related directly to binance websocket api), is receiving messages like this when listening to the diff depth stream: 

{
  "e": "depthUpdate", // Event type
  "E": 123456789,     // Event time
  "s": "BNBBTC",      // Symbol
  "U": 157,           // First update ID in event
  "u": 160,           // Final update ID in event
  "b": [              // Bids to be updated
    [
      "0.0024",       // price level to be updated
      "10",
      []              // ignore
    ]
  ],
  "a": [              // Asks to be updated
    [
      "0.0026",       // price level to be updated
      "100",          // quantity
      []              // ignore
    ]
  ]
}
==> if 'U' = 157 and 'u' = 160, I am assuming I should see 4 update messages. But here, only 1 bid and 1 ask have been updated. Am I wrong in my assumption? (this json is from [binance official doc](https://github.com/binance-exchange/binance-official-api-docs/blob/master/web-socket-streams.md#diff-depth-stream) but I've observed similar messages myself).

issue_title: Argument limit ignored in get_query_rows
issue_body: Method `get_query_rows` in `client.py` ignores the argument `limit` when `page_token` is not `None`. This goes against the intuitive behavior which would be to only return `limit` rows.

issue_title: when arraybuffer's byte length is 10**8, the connection is broken.
Objects in results arrays should not be nullable
issue_body:
This a minor but annoying issue when you use a front end tool like Apollo because it results in weaker typing.
It affects graphene-django as well, but I have more confidence in graphene-django-extras to fix it ! :-)
Here it is: results should not be of the form `results: [UserType]` but `results: [UserType!]` since returned objects in the array can't be null.
I don't know if it's somehow related, but the same issues applies to related objects resulting from foreign keys or M2M.

issue_title: Changes proposal
issue_body:
Hi there!

I found your module quite interesting and relevant for our needs. However, for our use case, I would propose a couple of updates/changes:
1. extending the model to include also additional info field, which would contain the message about the change (e.g. could be used as a summary of what is being changed and why). The point here to help users to digest the new terms. 
2. adding a template tag which would allow to display a modal with new terms, if such exists. This can be yet another means to communicate the new terms for users (e.g. by including the tag in the base template), an alternative to redirecting
3. as and extra-add on, to allow for multi-language support (at least for the info) field

If you accept the proposal, I could work towards a respective pull request. Alternatively, if you don't approve, I'm okey with introducing these changes in a separate repo. Let me know.
In any case, thanks a lot for your work and efforts! ;)

Regards,
Adrian

issue_title: Move the commonlib.parallel library into baselib
issue_body:
Inside the oq-hazardlib repository there is a package called `openquake.baselib`. The purpose of that package is to collect basic functionality independent from seismology. In particular the parallelization library, which has nothing to do with science, should go there. For historical reasons, it went into `openquake.commonlib` instead, in the oq-engine repository. This is the wrong place, since commonlib is meant to collect more specific utilities, for instance the parsers for NRML, which is a format specific to seismology.

Having `parallel` inside baselib means that users of hazardlib can write parallel programs without installing the engine. This was my original plan but in the past the parallelization library was not mature enough to be moved outside of the engine. Also, thanks to the recent improvements on our cluster, now the workers can access directly the input files in the controller node, so it is a lot easier to write parallel scripts. A scientist (say Graeme) can write a parallel script working on its machine and have it run *unchanged* on the cluster, making use of all the 256 cores!

Any abuse of this functionality will be punished by Daniele ;-)

issue_title: Using a filtered `region_grid` to compute the hazard for highly distributed exposures
issue_body:
For risk calculations involving exposures with assets distributed at a large number of sites, computing the hazard (gmfs / hazard curves) at each exposure site can be computationally expensive and indeed profligate.

A risk analyst can already actively address this issue by precomputing the hazard at a reduced set of sites by providing either: (1) a sites csv file with the list of reduced sites, or (2) a `region` and `region_grid_spacing`. 

The second option could be simplified by inferring the `region` directly from the exposure, and thus requiring the user to specify only the `region_grid_spacing`.

This option could also be enhanced by further discarding sites on the grid that are far away from any assets in the exposure.

The basic sequence of steps to achieve the above could be as follows — if the job configuration file specifies an exposure model and a `region_grid_spacing` but does not specify a `region`:
1. Compute the [convex hull](https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/blob/engine-2.9/openquake/hazardlib/geo/mesh.py#L343-L366) of all of the sites in the exposure, and use this set of points as the `region`
2. Use the `region_grid_spacing` to discretize the `region` into a grid
3. Discard sites from the grid that are farther away than a threshold value equal to `region_grid_spacing` / `sqrt(2)` from any asset in the exposure
4. Compute the hazard only on the sites on the grid that weren't discarded
5. Compute the risk for each asset using the hazard results from the nearest available site (nearest-neighbor interpolation).

issue_title: Try to remove the `.ext5` file
issue_body: This would allow to keep everything in a single file, as we always wanted. Despite evidence to the contrary gathered in the past, perhaps it is possible to read read concurrently and write sequentially on the datastore at the same time. In a single machine situation this seems to be possible if we fork from a closed datastore (see https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/pull/2715). In the past this approach did not work because we were forking starting from an open datastore.

issue_title: Proposal: Change format of `/v1/apps/<app id>/run`
issue_body:
Currently run returns a dynamic array (Ewww!) `[0, "hi\n"]`. The docs said that run returned `{"rc": 0, "output": "hi"}` which seems much more sensible to me.

I would like propose to change the response of run to the format suggested to the docs:

```json
{
  "rc": <return code>, 
  "output": "<command output>"
}

```

This is a backwards incompatible change, so this would probably warrant a API version bump to version 2.

The good news is that for [as long as we have had API docs](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/reference/api-v1.0/#run-one-off-commands) nobody has caught this huge difference, so it seems unlikely that many people are consuming that part of the api.

issue_title: Do we need a more sophisticated health check for deis-router?
issue_body:
This is unrelated to application health checks.

On each platform we support, load balancers are configured with a health check that just tests whether anything is listening on port 80.  This means any node in a Deis cluster that is running the `deis-router` reports back as "healthy" and is therefore included in that load balancer's pool of instances it can send traffic to.

Where this breaks down is the case where Kubernetes is the scheduler.  If the `deis-kube-proxy` component should fail on any given machine, then the consequence is that even though a router instance might be alive and well on that same machine, application traffic sent to that machine will never reach a pod.

Is it possible that `deis-router` should include a more sophisticated health check endpoint where a 200 is returned if the router is _able_ to route traffic?  Here, "able" is probably defined as the `deis-router` component is up and healthy, _and_, if applicable, `deis-kube-proxy` is as well.

Ping @bacongobbler for thoughts.

issue_title: remove scheduler tech previews for LTS release
issue_body: The experiment is finally over, and we are migrating Deis on top of kubernetes for Deis v2! We have had some users now still trying the tech previews in v1, which was never meant to be used in production nor is in any working state. For the LTS release, we should remove these tech previews so users won't be shooting themselves in the foot.

issue_title: can't import Contacts
Segmentation fault occurs in wrapped C lib, only when jnius is imported
issue_body:
I have a C library that I have wrapped with a C++ class, and cython. I get a segmentation fault when running one of the C functions, but only if I have imported jnius. If I don't import jnius the function returns just fine. Any idea what might be going on?

The C lib is [libpll](https://www.assembla.com/code/phylogenetic-likelihood-library/git/nodes/)
the wrapper is [here](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pllpy/0.1.10)
I'm using Red Hat Enterprise Linux Server release 6.3 (Santiago) kernel version 2.6.32-358.14.1.el6.x86_64

issue_title: Add android.ant_path to default.spec
issue_body:
I have kivy and ant installed from debian packages, buildozer from git because there is no debian package. Following a tutorial, I ran 
buildozer android debug deploy run
and noticed it wanted to download a new version of ant:
Android ANT is missing, downloading

Poking through the source of buildozer I discovered that it checks a config variable android.ant_path, but that variable wasn't in the buildozer.spec file it created for me. Since android.ndk_path and android.sdk_path were in the default spec, it would have been helpful if android.ant_path was too.

issue_title: How to correctly use Dataset module with LightFM.fit() method
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm dealing with a DB with a shape of:

UserId | ProductId | CountOfInteractions
------------ | ------------- | -------------
UserABC | ProductABD | 2
UserABC  | ProductZZZ | 12
... | ... |

I was playing around with the **Dataset** module for speeding it up the interactions and weights matrices building process, but I'm not sure if I'm getting it right. 

Its `.build_interactions()` function creates two matrices: 

- a binary one with userId-productId tuples set to 1 (if any interaction has occured in the training set) or 0 otherwise

- a weights matrix with userId-productId tuples set to the number of interactions between every tuple in the training set ("CountOfInteractions" field, in my example)

Now, if I'm getting right, I need to feed them into the `LightFM.fit()` method, as interactions and sample_weight arguments respectively.

My question is if it's the same thing if I build a sparse matrix of userId and productId count of interactions and feed it directly into the `LightFM.fit()`method as first argument of this function.

I'm getting very different embedding matrices when comparing the two methods, even if I use the same seed for the fitting process and I was wondering if I'm making some mistakes in one the above flows.

Thanks!

issue_title: Help debugging entry_points failure
issue_body: While trying to get pip to use the new `build_meta`, I ran into a problem: setuptools loaded the entry points from the global environment rather than the temporary build environment: [see here](https://travis-ci.org/xoviat/pip/jobs/288084422#L1036). How does `pkg_resources` discover `entry_points` and how can I override it?

issue_title: ez_setup.py incorrectly detect charset
issue_body:
The problem is reproduced when I am trying to init buildout by run bootstrap:
```
python bootstrap-buildout.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "bootstrap-buildout.py", line 119, in <module>
    ez['use_setuptools'](**setup_args)
  File "<string>", line 160, in use_setuptools
  File "<string>", line 375, in _resolve_version
  File "D:\bin\python35\lib\codecs.py", line 998, in getreader
    return lookup(encoding).streamreader
TypeError: lookup() argument must be str, not None

```

The bootstrap get latest ez_setup.py from https://bootstrap.pypa.io/ez_setup.py and try to exec it.

The real problem in the _resolve_version in ez_setup.py:
```
def _resolve_version(version):
...
    resp = urlopen(meta_url)  # https://pypi.org/pypi/setuptools/json
...
        try:
            charset = resp.info().get_content_charset()   #  returns None
        except Exception:
            # Python 2 compat; assume UTF-8
            charset = 'UTF-8'
        reader = codecs.getreader(charset) # fail here because charset is None
```
Possible last days Content-Type header was changed for https://pypi.org/pypi/setuptools/json.

Also I don't found repository for ez_setup.py. Only changelog contains info that it was removed from this repo, but there are no info about new location.

issue_title: Virtualenv seems to have overwritten system Python
issue_body:
Today I was trying to create a Python 2.7 virtualenv, but when I did, it replaced my Python 3.5.0 binaries with Python 2.7.10 binaries. Thereafter, I had no usable Python 3 on my system.

$ which python3.5
/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/bin/python3.5
$ python3.5 -c "import sys; print(sys.prefix)"
/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7
```

As you can see, I'm using OS X. I had installed Python 3.5.0 using the OS X installer from python.org. I had `alias python='python3'` in my shell. I also had originally had virtualenv installed in my Python 3.5 environment, but it wasn't working so I installed it to my Python 2.7 environment as a user package.

When I invoked virtualenv, I used something like `virtualenv --python python2.7 ../.env` where `virtualenv` probably resolved to the broken script in the Python 3 environment.

Unfortunately, I wasn't paying that much attention when it happened, so I'm unsure all the details. I'm going to try to replicate it, but for now, I just wanted to register this highly unusual and unexpected behavior.

development.rb

  config.cache_classes = false

issue_title: Make --cloud a global parameter for all commands
issue_body:
This feature request is similar to issue #5618 where a global --subscription parameter was added but request a global --cloud argument.  I'm adapting that request and borrowing heavily from it's text.

The current CLI currently requires this workflow:
```
# set cloud
az cloud set --name <cloud_name>

# do az stuff in that sub
az acs login --name ...
```
This makes it very unfriendly and dangerous for scripts and interactive use.

Consider:

Long running script sets curernt sub to SubProduction, then does some azure commands.
In the meantime user from the terminal, is thinking the commands execute against SubDev issues "delete resource group MyApp". OOPS.
If we had --cloud as a global flag, we could have all scripts use that and not interfere with each other.

I'd like a solution where `--cloud` could be added as a global flag so multiple commands could be run from a machine to simultaneously deploy to multiple clouds/regions.

issue_title: FeatureRequest: ability to set retry option in storage
issue_body:
### Which service(blob, file, queue) does this issue concern?
queue

### Which version of the SDK was used? Please provide the output of `pip freeze`.
azure-storage==0.36.0

### What problem was encountered?
No option to set the RetryOptions for QueueService object.
QueueService inherits from StorageClient class which has retry set to default ExponentialRetry in its init.
In pydocumentdb, we can set RetryOptions as property of client's connection policy. It would be great if that could be unified across storage service as well.

### Have you found a mitigation/solution?
No.

Note: for table service, please post the issue here instead: https://github.com/Azure/azure-cosmosdb-python.

### Steps to Reproduce
<!-- If the current behavior is a bug, please provide specific, minimal steps to independently reproduce.
     Include the exact conda commands that reproduce the issue and their output between the ticks below. -->
Create a conda package with the following recipe (although the recipe is incorrect, nothing complained):
```
package:
  name: scratch
  version: 1.0.0

build:
  features:
    -
```

## Expected Behavior
<!-- What do you think should happen? -->

1. conda-build should complain that value of a feature can't be blank
2. conda shouldn't go into infinite recursion

The issue happens because somehow conda passes `*[track_features=]` to push_MatchSpec() in resolve.py, which leads to infinite recursion with push_MatchSpec() being passed '*' again and again.

FWIW, conda 4.3* doesn't suffer this problem and happily installs the package.

issue_title: default `default_channels` set based on `sys.platform` instead of `subdir`
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report (?) - [x] feature request ## Current Behavior `context.default_channels` is set to ```python DEFAULT_CHANNELS = DEFAULT_CHANNELS_WIN if bool(sys.platform == "win32") else DEFAULT_CHANNELS_UNIX ``` on default. That means it's hardcoded wrt `sys.platform` instead of being set based on the configured `subdir`. To reproduce Windows users' package plans, it would be convenient to get the default Windows channels when one uses `CONDA_SUBDIR=win-64 conda create ...`. ### Steps to Reproduce ``` $ CONDA_SUBDIR=win-64 conda config --show default_channels default_channels: - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro ``` ```bash (base) $ python -c 'import conda.base.constants as c; print(*c.DEFAULT_CHANNELS_WIN, sep="\n")' https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/msys2 ``` ## Expected Behavior ```bash $ CONDA_SUBDIR=win-64 conda config --show default_channels default_channels: - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro - https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/msys2 ``` ## Environment Information

issue_title: Including conda build recipe with package source and single-sourcing version data
issue_body:
It seems reasonable that the conda build recipe should be included in a project's source repo, and indeed it looks like a few conda projects [use this approach](https://github.com/conda/conda-env/tree/develop/conda.recipe). In addition, verison information for a python project should be kept in a single location; the [Python Packaging User Guide gives a few strategies](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/single_source_version.html).

Is it possible to single-source a package's version data and include the conda build recipe in the same repo?

issue_title: out-of-chronology releases cause headaches
issue_body:
It is typical that consecutive releases for a specific mainline of the product will be following chronologically, but it's not a good absolute assumption.

It was observed that sometimes an earlier commit (in the version tree) might actually be tagged and released after a later one which is still a valid situation.

Example:

```
$ grep "^\*" makelib-1.10.0/makelib.spec
* Mon May 14 2018 Waldek Maleska <waldek.maleska@nokia.com> - 1.10.0-146437
[...]
* Wed Nov 22 2017 Red Acted <red.acted@nokia.com> - 1.5.0-20
* Mon Nov 27 2017 Red Acted <red.acted@nokia.com> - 1.4.0-19
* Thu Nov 23 2017 IRed Acted <red.acted@nokia.com> - 1.4.0-18
[...]

$ rpmbuild  -ba makelib.spec [....]
...
Building target platforms: noarch
Building for target noarch
error: %changelog not in descending chronological order   
Makefile:82: recipe for target 'dist/makelib-1.10.0-redacted.noarch.rpm' failed
```


Possible implementation ideas for sorting the releases:

- by date unconditionally
- by date based on flag (on by default), e.g. `--sort-by-date`
- allow sorting by a predefined set of fields, e.g. date, tag: `--sort-by={date|tag}`

Personally I lean towards option (3), but it's up to debate. Input welcome.

issue_title: Data structures
issue_body:
@alexcjohnson 
Hi Alex, I noticed you mentioned that one of the next steps will be to improve data saving so I thought I'd share my ideas on it before everything is made and fully integrated. One of the things I want to do after the data saving is move over our analysis and/or built a framework for easily analysing data. Automation of analysis of experiments is one of the things I have in mind when talking about data structures but is a subject that is large enough to warrant it's own discussion. 

With respect to data structures I think there are a few aspects that are relevant 
- Structure of data in the file 
- data format (JSON, HDF5, data-server or other)  
- file-handling and interfaces (default directories, search and load functions etc) 
### Structure of the data file

I like the current structure a lot, I think the way a dataset can be passed to a plot and contains all the metadata. However when doing analysis I find that I require some more functionality. 
To be specific, I require 
- A snapshot of the settings at the time of the experiment 
- The ability to store additional data in the file (fit-results, rotated and normalised data etc) 
- Other metadata (commit hash of our personal code, datatime etc) 

The way we address this currently is by having a hierarchical data structure that makes a distinction between a dataset (array's of values + labels) and the datafile (contains different groups of which the dataset is one). I would propose to use a similar hierarchical format for Qcodes. 
### Data format

We have had a lot of discussions on our side with respect to the data format. Currently JSON is the default and we (in Delft) are more used to HDF5, let me just summarise the pros and cons as I see them with the goal of making our use of both better. I guess as long as there is a good data handler the underlying backend does not really matter (similar to how we are with plotting now) but I guess the default will significantly impact how we think about it. 
@cdickel , I know you are pro HDF5 can you go over this and see if there is anything I missed/you want to add? 
- Pro JSON 
  - Simple/text based/openable in any editor 
  -  Native support for dictionaries (we use a workaround in HDF5) 
  -  current QCodes default 
- Pro H5PY/HDF5 
  -  HDF5 Hierarchical data-viewer (allows browsing datafile and folding of folders, looked around but did not find equivalent for JSON) 
  - File-sizes (this is a real problem when taking single shot data for long measurements) 
  - Possible to write/extend a dataset that is open, prevents data loss when experiment crashes 
  - Easily extract data in hierarchical fashion (no string parsing or custom import functions)
  - Can easily add new data groups to an existing file  
  - Delft default (admittedly only a pro for us) 
- Alternatives
  - If anyone knows of any alternatives please let me know 

I would be very willing to switch to JSON but to me it does not offer the same as HDF5 at this point. I would love to have some discussion on this. 
### Interface/file-handling

We currently use a format that saves any datafile as follows: `user_datadirectory/YYMMDD/HHMMSS_label/HHMMSS_label.hdf5`. The `user_directory` is a global variable that is set upon initialisation of the environment (similar to creating an instrument upon init). This directory typically does not change for several months. The label is a non-unique identifier for an experiment such as e.g. `T1_qubit_3`, we typically do the same run multiple times and do not want to worry about coming up with unique names, the timestamp ensures uniqueness. The file is nested in a folder with the same name to allow saving figures that come out of the analysis next to it. 

On the analysis side we have functions that look for data files in one of three ways. If a label is specified it looks for the last file that has that label, e.g. `T1` would find our `T1_qubit_3` file. If a timestamp is specified it uses this as a unique identifier for a file. If nothing is specified it will default to the last generated file. On top of that we have a whole bunch of higher level functions. 

This scheme is heavily qtlab inspired and works pretty well for us. Some of the drawbacks we have encountered are: 
- timestamp is not unique when doing very short experimental runs (sometimes multiple start within the same second) 
- Only possible to pick one user_datadirectory 

I'd be interested in what your ( @alexcjohnson )  plans are for this kind of interface and file/folder handling. I understand that you currently have to specify all this when using the Loop but I have to admit I have not used that in a while. 

@cdickel, Can you let me know what you think of all this? I tried to incorporate ideas from our discussions over the last year in it but I am sure I have missed some. 
@damazter , what do you think? You guys do things slightly different so I am curious.

issue_title: Missing Layer normalization: no operator for variance (or mean_and_variance)
issue_body:
MXNet should support [Layer Normalization](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1607.06450.pdf) out-of-the-box, either by having an operator for it, or provide support operators that make it easier.
While it is possible to do with current MXNet ([see here](https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/master/sockeye/layers.py#L18)), it is a bit cumbersome, quite slow, and fairly tricky with shape inference due to the many broadcast_* ops.

In general it would be useful to have an operator to compute the variance, with a signature similar to ``mean``.
One could also think about  a ``mean_and_variance`` operator, similar to [tf.moments](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/nn/moments) that computes both statistics jointly which is likely more efficient.

Regarding a full Layer Normalization op it could look as follows:

LayerNormalization on dimension 3:
```
# output = scale * (inputs - mean) / sqrt(var + eps) + shift
output = mx.sym.LayerNormalization(data=input, axis=d3)
```
where the variables are created if not passed (similar to FullyConnected).

One should also be able to pass existing variables:
```
output = mx.sym.LayerNormalization(
  data=input,
  axis=d3,
  scale=mx.sym.Variable('scale'),
  shift=mx.sym.Variable('shift'),
  eps=1e-9)
```

In contrast to Batch normalization it is not necessary to compute running means or store anything from training for inference.

issue_title: get_comment_list is list, not queryset
issue_body:
When using `get_comment_list` [this line](https://github.com/django/django-contrib-comments/blob/master/django_comments/templatetags/comments.py#L113) converts the queryset of comments into a Python list. This caused a bug in my threaded-comments code, here's why:

I'm using `django-mptt` to render threaded comments and their code expects depth-first ordered nodes for their `recursetree` templatetag. For the nodes to be ordered depth-first, `django-mptt` [checks if the given list of nodes is a queryset](https://github.com/django-mptt/django-mptt/blob/master/mptt/templatetags/mptt_tags.py#L255) and if so, applies the necessary ordering. Since the list is not a queryset and the list is obviously not guaranteed to be in depth-first order, the algorithm fails.

I don't know what consequences it would have to just return a queryset from `get_comment_list` (they mostly behave the same and it would keep evaluation just a bit more lazy). Furthermore, even though the name says "list", I kinda expected a queryset, simply because that would be more Django-style.

As a workaround I set up my own template tag `get_comment_queryset` which returns the `queryset` directly. If changing `get_comment_list` is a no-go, maybe adding a `get_comment_queryset` templatetag might be an option. But I guess this issue shall just serve future threaded comment bug hunters as a reference.

issue_title: dotnet core web api exception handling - unhandled exceptions
Latest version has Syntax Error in core.py on Python 2.6
issue_body:
When I import from  `dyn.tm.session`, I get a syntax error:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "dyn-python-test.py", line 1, in <module>
        from dyn.tm.session import DynectSession
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.6/site-packages/dyn/tm/session.py", line 8, in <module>
        from ..core import SessionEngine
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.6/site-packages/dyn/core.py", line 23
        return {x: dict_obj[x] for x in dict_obj if dict_obj[x] is not None and
                                 ^
    SyntaxError: invalid syntax

I am using Python 2.6.5:

    $ python --version
    Python 2.6.5

and `dyn-python-1.1.0`

It looks like dict comprehension is a Python 2.7 feature:
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0274/

However, the documentation says:
"Requires Python 2.6 or higher, or the "simplejson" package."

I suspect that a simple fix would be to change that line to this:

    return dict((x, dict_obj[x]) for x in dict_obj if dict_obj[x] is not None and
            not hasattr(dict_obj[x], '__call__'))

However, after doing that it looks like there are other places that are also not Python 2.6 compatible.

So, it might be best to just update the docs to let everyone know that it is not compatible with Python 2.6.

issue_title: Indented tables are ignored

When the table is indented (e.g. inside an unordered list), it's ignored.

  |---|---|
  | value1 | value2 |
  | value3 | value4 |

* Marked doesn't work with indented tables like this:

  | column1 | column2 |
  |---|---|
  | value1 | value2 |
  | value3 | value4 |

issue_title: Closing a stream causes recvmmsg to read from closed file descriptor
issue_body:
This is because the usual safety guarantees in asio for closing a socket while there is an outstanding asynchronous operation don't work when calling recvmmsg oneself. Normally this is a harmless warning, but a race condition could cause an unrelated file/socket to be opened with the same file descriptor, causing data to be consumed from the wrong place.

I'm not quite sure how to fix this. Possibly one can duplicate the FD so that the socket doesn't really close until the reader is destroyed - but need to check how that interacts with asio.

issue_title: Installation broken on Fedora because other lib structures
issue_body:
On fedora after installing libdwarf-devel (and other from family), libdwarf.h and dwarf.h are located in `/usr/include/libdwarf` not in `/usr/include/`, so the installation crashes:
`src/hotpatch/elf.c:9:19: fatal error: dwarf.h: No such file or directory`

After copying both files level up, then it crashes:
```src/hotpatch/elf.c:15:25: fatal error: libelf/gelf.h: No such file or directory```
`gelf.h` is NOT in subdirectory, copying file to `libelf` subdir solves the problem.


below are packages contents
```

/usr/include/libdwarf
/usr/include/libdwarf/dwarf.h
/usr/include/libdwarf/libdwarf.h

```

issue_title: Table.truncate fails
issue_body:
Table.truncate() has a default parameter `mode=None`, which overrides the default of `''` in SQLAdapter.truncate(), and hence produces a query like:

    TRUNCATE TABLE TablenameNone

i.e. appending the `None` to the tablename. Of course that leads to a 

    ProgrammingError: (1146, "Table 'TablenameNone' doesn't exist")

Shouldn't the default in Table.truncate() be `''` as well then? Or does one have to specify a `mode=''` explicitly for Table.truncate()?

issue_title: SimpleConsumer may allow consume() after stop()
issue_body: This issue was raised as part of #215 - if there's still a message on some queue, `consume()` will return that instead of raising a `ConsumerStoppedException`. That's an issue because there won't be an offset auto-committer at that point.

issue_title: Producer tests share consumer between each other
issue_body:
The current producer test instantiates a single consumer for all tests. This means that each test is expecting all other tests to consume all messages it produces. This means if a test fails it can cause unrelated tests to fail.

https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/tests/pykafka/test_producer.py#L27

Perhaps, instantiate a new consumer at the largest offset for each test. 

Managing this kind of dependencies and cleanup is perfect for py.test fixtures. Although i have never used fixtures for class based tests.

issue_title: Streamparse 3.0.0 not compatible with Storm 1.0.1
issue_body:
Hello,

the currently stable streamparse 3.0.0 is not compatible with Storm 1.0.1, which is the latest official release available on storm.apache.org. It requires at least Storm 1.0.2 or 1.1.0, because `FluxShellSpout.setNamedStream` and `FluxShellBolt.setNamedStream` are [only available since then](https://github.com/apache/storm/blob/v1.0.2/external/flux/flux-wrappers/src/main/java/org/apache/storm/flux/wrappers/bolts/FluxShellBolt.java#L100).

Since 1.0.2 and 1.1.0 are only [released on GitHub](https://github.com/apache/storm/releases/tag/v1.0.2) and not yet available on maven this might be a serious problem. Streamparse also uses 1.0.1 as a default in the examples.

issue_title: sparse submit fails when config.json specifies multiple environments
Support for independently creating/modifying product images.
issue_body:
Please correct me if I'm wrong, but at the moment the only way to create/update product images through the Python API is directly through a POST/PUT call to the product endpoint.

As per the [Shopify API documentation on product images](http://docs.shopify.com/api/product_image), we should be able to make POST and PUT calls directly to `/admin/products/#{id}/images.json` and `PUT /admin/products/#{id}/images/#{id}.json`. However, this doesn't seem to work currently with the API as the prefix for the `Image` class isn't properly being built. Thus, something like this...

```python
image = shopify.Image()
image.src = "http://example.com/example.png"
image.product_id = 123456789
image.save()
```

... tries to POST to `/admin/products//images.json` which returns a 406.

Looking at the source, I think a fix would be to implement the `prefix()` class method, similar to what's been done with the `Variant` class. Just wanted to run this by folks who might be more familiar with this to see if that might be a valid solution.

issue_title: get_query_set patching is very broken
issue_body:
The method included to patch get_query_set is very broken, and leads to some very bug code in circumstances where managers get subclassed - the behaviour of the subclass can be skipped. This is a very serious error for the case of Related managers, because it means that the limiting of the queryset that is added by the subclass get_query_set method gets skipped, so the related manager queries apply to all objects.

I've created a branch that demonstrates the problem, adding 4 tests, 2 of which fails with Django 1.8, one fails with Django 1.5:

https://github.com/spookylukey/django-compat/tree/get_query_set_patch_bug

I've created a PR so that you can see the tests failing.

The full details are explained in this blog post:

http://lukeplant.me.uk/blog/posts/handling-django's-get_query_set-rename-is-hard/

issue_title: Custom Authorizer Function ARN within Swagger File
issue_body:
We have the following scenario: 

We want with single Cloud Formation stack to deploy an API Gateway having methods secured with Lambda based Custom Authorizer. The API must be defined with Swagger file.

The Custom Authorizer Lambda must be created within the same Cloud Formation stack.  The question is if or how we can reference the Custom Authorizer Lambda function within the Swagger file?

For example:

```
securityDefinitions:
  OktaCustomAuthorizer:
    type: "apiKey"
    name: "Authorization"
    in: "header"
    x-amazon-apigateway-authtype: "custom"
    x-amazon-apigateway-authorizer:
      authorizerResultTtlInSeconds: 300
      authorizerUri: "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/<<FUNCTION_ARN>>/invocations"
      type: "token"
```

If Authorizer Lambda have been created previously, we can statically reference it in the construct above. However, what if Custom Authorizer function is part of the current stack? The API method Lambda request integrations can handle these kind of scenarios with stage variables substitutions, i.e. ${stageVariables.LambdaFunctionName}. But for Custom Authorizers it is not the case.

Any opinion? The requirement for having everything in one stack seems very reasonable.

Best Regards,

I would also include a key path there, it is needed in most usecases (`ssl_certificate_key` in nginx). Now, I have to manually change `fullchain.pem` to `privkey.pem` in the cert path.

I think the following format would be way more usable:
```
 - Congratulations! Your certificate and chain have been saved at:
   /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/fullchain.pem
   Key file has been saved at:
   /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/privkey.pem
   Your cert will expire on 2666-06-06. To obtain a new or tweaked
   version of this certificate in the future, simply run
   certbot-auto again. To non-interactively renew *all* of your
   certificates, run "certbot-auto renew"
```

What do you think?

issue_title: certbot broke an apache vhost by not paying attention/warning about vhost-SAN needs
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
RHEL6

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
certbot-auto

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
```sh
./certbot-auto
...
Which names would you like to activate HTTPS for?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
{7 domains listed}
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Select the appropriate numbers separated by commas and/or spaces, or leave input
blank to select all options shown (Enter 'c' to cancel): 3
Obtaining a new certificate
Performing the following challenges:
...
Deploying Certificate for ... to VirtualHost /etc/httpd/conf.d/001-test-ssl.conf
...
Please choose whether or not to redirect HTTP traffic to HTTPS, removing HTTP access.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: No redirect - Make no further changes to the webserver configuration.
2: Redirect - Make all requests redirect to secure HTTPS access. Choose this for
new sites, or if you're confident your site works on HTTPS. You can undo this
change by editing your web server's configuration.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Select the appropriate number [1-2] then [enter] (press 'c' to cancel): 2
Redirecting vhost in /etc/httpd/conf.d/001-test.conf to ssl vhost in /etc/httpd/conf.d/001-test-ssl.conf
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
It broke my SAN certificate (which held 2 of the 7 domains) and was used by the vhost that it was editing

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
###### Logs are stored in `/var/log/letsencrypt` by default. Feel free to redact domains, e-mail and IP addresses as you see fit.
(Available if necessary)

## Here is the relevant nginx server block or Apache virtualhost for the domain I am configuring:
```
<VirtualHost *:80>
        Servername test.example.com
        ServerAlias example.example.org
        ServerAlias test
        ServerAlias example
</VirtualHost>
```

Essentially, by asking me a question about domains (1..7) instead of vhosts (1..4), certbot enabled me to replace a working SAN cert (which was configured for 2 of the 7 domains) for a vhost with a single domain cert (one of the 7 domains) for the vhost which broke the other domain.

When using the apache widget, it needs to be aware of related certs and should suggest converting them as sets. If a user asks to do something that would break a vhost, they should probably be told to edit the vhost first. if someone asks for all of the domains in a vhost + more, that's totally fine, since that wouldn't break a vhost, and could be useful if one is planning to expand...

issue_title: Add a notebook cell level magic system
issue_body:
The IPython kernel has a magic system which can be used to do quite a lot of interesting things. It would be nice if that concept can be extended to the notebook.

Having the magics only in IPython has the problem that you can't use it to call things in the stack which are higher up, like creating new kernels and running code on them or building magics which work in julia and python.

I don't want line magics, only cell based ones. This could be realized by looking for `"#!% "` at the start of the cell and if found get the second word and look for a magic (=func) of that name and call it via `func(<cell object>, <notebook session>)` (notebook session would give access to the kernel manager and so on, cell would give access to both the content of the cell as to the representation to e.g. hide the inputs). It would need an extension point where extensions could add such functions (`add_magic(name, func)`).

An example magic would be `#!% run_on_kernel ir\n<lots of R code>`, which would open an R kernel (if not already existing in this session), run the code on that kernel and display the result.

Another example would be extending the display system https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/652

issue_title: Refactor tree handling outside of yaml and json stores
issue_body: Since we have to allow output formats different from the input ones (i.e., input yaml, output json), it would be easier to assume sops can only encrypt trees, as the Python version does. The Go version currently does not make such assumption and lets `Store`s do whatever they want, but by doing this, we'd need each store to create a function to port their data to and from every other store. It's easier to only support trees.

issue_title: SDR Classifier and region don't handle multiple calls correctly
issue_body:
The SDR classifier updates `_patternNZHistory` each time `compute` is called. But clients are calling `compute` multiple times per record. This is because there are sometimes multiple categories to learn for each record.

We should support this either by allowing multiple categories to be specified to `compute` or by enabling multiple calls to `compute` with only a single update to `_patternNZHistory`.

cc @ywcui1990

issue_title: ImageFolder Dataset: `make_dataset` with the same (sorted) order.
issue_body:
When creating the dataset using `ImageFolder`, different machines can have different orders of images. 

This may not be an issue for the training set, but when I want to compare the validation results among different machines (especially when I want to ensemble the softmax outputs of validation set), different orders of images can be annoying.

A simple modification can solve the problem: currently the `make_dataset` function uses `for target in os.listdir(dir):`, we can simply change it to `for target in sorted(os.listdir(dir)):`.

Since the loading procedure can be shuffled, I have not found creating the dataset with a sorted order has any drawbacks. Actually, it will be much easier for the users when they want to ensemble the outputs of the full dataset among different machines (where `os.listdir(dir)` might produce different orders of results`).

issue_title: Routing tasks to different assistant pools
issue_body:
We are interested in starting to use the assistant functionality, however we need a way to have different pools of assistants that pick up only certain tasks. For example, we want to dedicate a pool of X number of assistant processes to work on a background pipeline, and then have other pool(s) dedicated to other efforts, such as a user running an ad-hoc job.

It is possible the existing `resources` feature could be leveraged and just have a single pool. It is not as flexible though, for example does not allow customization such as different configuration.

Maybe something like an `--assistant-pool` argument which would be specified on both the submitting worker and the assistant side?

One thing that is a little odd is that when tasks overlap from two submissions to different pools, the task actually could be run in either pool. I think this is OK though -- it is no different from the caveats standard worker case.

issue_title: subscription.attributes lists nonexistant plan_code
issue_body:
the .attributes property for subscription objects includes attributes that don't actually exist on the object.  I've seen this in other objects, too, so I don't think it's specific to that model.

For example, 'plan_code' is listed in the subscription model's "attributes" list, but a subscription object has no 'plan_code' attribute.

Therefore, the following fails:

    account = recurly.Account.get('some_email@example.com')
    subscriptions = account.subscriptions()
    sub = subscriptions[0]
    'plan_code' in sub.attributes # returns true
    sub.plan_code # throws attribute error

However, the plan_code *is* available for this object by the following mechanism:

`sub.plan.plan_code`

A break in the attributes list means that you can't really do anything dynamic with the attributes list, like create a dictionary with the object:

`dict((attrib, getattr(sub, attrib)) for attrib in sub.attributes)`

As a result, it's impossible to create a general mechanism for transforming a Recurly object.  With the dictionary example, it means that it's impossible to create a mechanism to render arbitrary Recurly objects in a web page; you would have to write a custom render function for every class exposed by the API if you wanted to do that.

The attributes list appears to be used erroneously by the client library itself.  For example, the .to_element method generates an xml.etree.ElementTree.Element object, but fails silently at generating elements for the attributes in the attribute list that do not really exist.  As a result, if you use .to_element on a subscription object, the Element instance that is returned is not representative of what the API returns.  WIth the example of subscriptions, the resulting .to_element Element contains no reference back to the Subscription Plan that the user has subscribed to:

    In [10]: e = sub.to_element()

    In [11]: list(e)
    Out[11]: 
    [<Element 'uuid' at 0x102f48e90>,
     <Element 'state' at 0x102f48ed0>,
     <Element 'quantity' at 0x102f48f10>,
     <Element 'activated_at' at 0x102f48f50>,
     <Element 'canceled_at' at 0x102f485d0>,
     <Element 'expires_at' at 0x102f48650>,
     <Element 'current_period_started_at' at 0x102f48cd0>,
     <Element 'current_period_ends_at' at 0x102f48d90>,
     <Element 'trial_started_at' at 0x102f3abd0>,
     <Element 'trial_ends_at' at 0x102f3ab50>,
     <Element 'unit_amount_in_cents' at 0x102f3a610>,
     <Element 'total_billing_cycles' at 0x102f3aad0>,
     <Element 'currency' at 0x102f4a0d0>,
     <Element 'subscription_add_ons' at 0x102f4a150>,
     <Element 'account' at 0x102f4a1d0>]

issue_title: Setting the timeout to 0 delivers an immediate timeout on Linux, but is ignored on Windows
issue_body:
I noticed by accident that the behaviour of
```
(set-option :TIMEOUT 0)
```
differs on Linux and Windows Z3 binaries. On Windows, this becomes an infinite timeout, whereas on Linux it's really a zero timeout (every query is immediately cancelled). From a quick guess at the code, I think the Windows version behaves the way it does is because CreateTimerQueueTimer() is called with a period of zero, which means that it's a one-shot timer, and the first callback is ignored.

I noticed this because Boogie sets the timeout to zero by default if you don't specify one, and evidently they are expecting it to be infinite.

issue_title: Unexpected results from `str.indexof` with offset argument
issue_body:
When I do
```
(declare-const action String)
(assert (>= (str.indexof action "abc" 1) 0))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
I get
```
sat
(model 
  (define-fun action () String
    "")
)
```
which doesn't seem right. I would expect a solution to look like "?abc" or "???abc" or something like that. Indeed, checking that I understood the semantics of `str.indexof` when the offset argument is bigger than the length of the string itself, I tried substituting `""` for `action` and
```
(declare-const action String)
(assert (>= (str.indexof "" "abc" 1) 0))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
yields `unsat`, as I would expect.

issue_title: Missing interpretation for constant in model output by (get-model)
issue_body:
The code:

```
(declare-const xp Int)
(assert (> xp 10))
(assert  (forall ((x Int) (y Int))
               (=> (< x (- y xp))
                 (exists ((z Int))
	           (and (< x z)
	    	        (< z y))))))
(check-sat)
(get-model)

```
outputs:

```
sat
(model 
)

```
The model is empty, even if it should give an interpretation to xp.

By removing the first assertion, `(assert (> xp 10))`, the model is output as expected.
